summary: Dan Lewis was torn between going into teaching/coaching and management consulting after graduating, unsure which path he actually wanted to pursue. A dean at Yale introduced him to the idea of "option value": evaluate the choice not by which he preferred in the moment, but by which path kept the other option open longest. The dean's logic was that going into consulting first left the odds of later becoming a teacher high, while teaching first left the odds of later breaking into consulting low. That reframing — pick whichever option preserves the other option, not just the one you like more, when you're genuinely unsure — became his actual decision rule and is a reusable heuristic for any two-path career fork.
quote: he basically said, if you go do management consulting right now, you can have, the odds of you being able to go be a teacher later are really high. Yeah. If you go do a teaching right now, the odds of being able to go into management consulting later are really low. So, if you're not sure which one you want to do, pick the one that has more option value.

summary: Before Convoy had a finished product, Dan Lewis set a concrete numeric goal: reach 100 shipments, purely so the company would have enough real operating data to tell investors a credible story for a Series A raise. Rather than rallying the team around an abstract mission, this specific, countable target created urgency and alignment, and it directly enabled an aggressive fundraising timeline — going from company founding to a credible Series A pitch in about eight months. The insight is that a concrete, even somewhat arbitrary, numeric milestone motivates a team and drives execution in a way a vague mission statement doesn't.
quote: we're, we're in trucking. We were new. Like, we need a hundred shipments, um, by this date. We didn't have a product. Like, we need to get a hundred shipments at least so we can understand our business enough to go have a really credible story about how to take it to the next level.

summary: Many startups try to write their company values on day one. Dan Lewis asked Jeff Bezos, a Convoy investor, when and how Amazon created its leadership principles, expecting an early foundational exercise. Instead, Bezos said Amazon deliberately waited until the company had grown to roughly 90-100 people, then looked back at what had actually made them successful and only then codified those observed behaviors into formal values — rather than inventing aspirational values upfront and hoping the culture would conform to them. Convoy copied this approach directly, waiting about a year before drafting its own values based on what had genuinely worked for the business rather than what sounded good early on.
quote: we didn't just go set the values. We, the company developed and then we saw what was working, right? And then we thought about, okay, what's working, now let's capture those and encapsulate those values into a set of things that we actually live by.

summary: At Amazon, meetings don't use slide decks. Instead someone writes a six-page narrative Word document, and when the group convenes, the first roughly 30 minutes of a one-hour meeting are spent reading the document in silence before any discussion begins — the remaining time is spent debating the actual substance rather than sitting through a scripted pitch. Dan Lewis's takeaway is that a narrative document is far harder to "mask" weak thinking behind than a PowerPoint, where formatting and bullet points can paper over gaps in logic, so the format itself forces higher-quality, more transparent thinking rather than just being a stylistic preference.
quote: they don't do PowerPoints, right? It's a Word document. So you walk in, it's the six-pager, you write a document, you walk in, it reads it in silence before the meeting starts... You can mask things in a PowerPoint, you can mask things in a presentation, you can't mask them in a Word document.

summary: At most companies, individual team goals get rolled up and aggregated as they move up the org chart — Dan Lewis cites Microsoft as an example, where seven team goals roll into one higher goal, several of those roll into another, and by the time it reaches the CEO he's tracking only 15-20 blended goals. Amazon does the opposite: goals from small teams of 10-20 people flow all the way to the top without aggregation, so senior leadership is literally tracking hundreds of individual team goals rather than a compressed summary. The counterintuitive effect is that teams feel more accountable, not less, because they know a specific senior leader could actually be watching their exact number rather than a diluted rollup that no one individual owns.
quote: the goals that a lot of individual teams have, maybe a 10-person team or a 20-person team has, roll all the way up to the top. They don't get aggregated... at Microsoft, for example... these seven goals were rolling to this goal. And then... the CEO would have like 15 or 20 goals. And so there was kind of a watered-down effect.

summary: Dan Lewis uses Amazon's product-reviews feature as a cautionary example of how naming a team after its solution quietly narrows what that team is able to build. Amazon's original problem was customer confidence — how to help shoppers feel safe buying something they can't touch or ask a salesperson about — and reviews were just one solution to that problem. But once the team was literally named "Reviews," every hire and every goal became about growing reviews (more reviews, more countries, less fraud), and the team stopped asking what else could solve the original confidence problem. Only when Amazon deliberately revisited the original problem did new solutions emerge, like customer Q&A and "does this run big or small" fit guidance. Convoy has adopted this as an explicit cultural check: when someone is deep in a solution, the team is expected to ask what problem it was actually solving in the first place.
quote: if you forget about the problem you're originally solving and you focus on improving the solution, you may actually miss like five other amazing opportunities... Every time we're working on product and process, we need to remember... what was the problem you started with?

summary: Convoy's stated biggest mistake was assuming that simply giving truck drivers company-issued phones would work as an operational solution — an assumption that broke down once actually deployed with real drivers. The interview cuts off before Dan Lewis finishes explaining the specific reason it failed, but the core lesson still stands: don't assume that equipping an operationally distinct workforce, like truck drivers whose device habits and needs differ from office employees, with standard company hardware will automatically translate into adoption. It's a caution against treating a seemingly straightforward logistics or tooling decision as safe without validating it against how the actual end users operate day to day.
quote: we assumed we could give drivers phones... Turns out that you can't just give drivers phones because they don't, unlike, we thought
